meta.title: Z4 E86 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW Z4 Coupé E86 engine — M54, N52, S54 M Coupé. Real UK costs and honest reliability data. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW Z4 E86 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned E86 engine typically costs £1,800–£3,600 supply only [BMW-QUOTE], depending on the engine — the M54 2.5i sits at £1,800–£3,200, the N52 3.0si at £2,000–£3,600, and the S54 Z4 M at £3,000–£3,400. Fitting adds £520–£1,040. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Z4 E86 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N52B30 (3.0si) is the E86's most reliable engine — a magnesium-alloy straight-six with no turbocharger or direct-injection issues. It's smooth, responsive, and has a proven track record. The M54 2.5i is also robust but older. The S54 is high-strung and requires diligent maintenance — it's not unreliable, just expensive.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW Z4 E86?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N52 suffers from oil leaks (cam cover, oil filter housing) and VANOS issues due to worn sealing rings. The electric water pump is a known failure point on both N52 and M54. The S54 (Z4 M) requires valve clearance checks every second service and has VANOS and rod bearing concerns. Rusty brake lines and electrical gremlins affect all models.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW Z4 E86 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the E86 is based on proven underpinnings and is generally well-regarded for reliability. The M54 and N52 are both durable engines. The S54 is more demanding but fundamentally strong. Regular maintenance is key — these are now 15+ year old cars, so gaskets, seals, and cooling system components need attention.

mainEntity.name: Which E86 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but a Z4 M without documented valve adjustment, VANOS, or rod bearing history is a financial risk. A neglected N52 without cooling system history is also a gamble. Avoid any car with obvious overheating damage or poor service history. All are worth owning if well-maintained.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my E86 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Oil leaks or burning smell (N52 gaskets), rough idle or loss of power (VANOS), overheating (cooling system failure), or knocking from the bottom end (S54 rod bearings) are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an E86 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a £10,000 3.0si, a £2,000–£3,600 N52 recon is 20–36% — well within our 70% BMW threshold. Against a £6,000 2.5i, a £1,800–£3,200 M54 recon is 30–53% — borderline but often viable for a cherished example. For a Z4 M at £18,000+, a £3,000–£3,400 S54 recon is 17–19% — a straightforward repair decision. These are appreciating modern classics; replacement costs are manageable.

mainEntity.name: How long do E86 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M54 and N52 can comfortably exceed 150,000 miles with proper servicing — the cooling system is the limiting factor. The S54 is capable of high mileage but requires more frequent maintenance — valve adjustments, rod bearings, and VANOS work should be budgeted for at 40k–80k mile intervals.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the E86 and E85 Z4?

acceptedAnswer.text: The E86 is the coupé version of the first-generation Z4 (2006–2008). The E85 is the roadster (2002–2008). They share the same platform, engines, and mechanicals, but the E86 has a fixed roof that adds structural rigidity, improves handling, and gives a more focused driving experience. The E86 is rarer — only about 17,000 coupés were built worldwide. Both are excellent, but the E86 is more collectable.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my E86 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine code is also stamped on the cylinder block. For petrol: M54B25 (2.5i), N52B30 (3.0si), S54B32 (Z4 M Coupé). Tell us your variant and registration year and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted BMW specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the E86 coupé — from the 2.5-litre M54 straight-six and the 3.0-litre N52 to the S54-powered Z4 M Coupé that shared its heart with the E46 M3. Real UK replacement costs and honest reliability data for BMW's most focused modern coupé. Backed by real UK enquiries [BMW-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: BMW Z4 Coupé E86 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the E86 coupé specifically — including the M54 2.5-litre, the N52 3.0-litre in various states of tune, and the S54 from the E46 M3 that powered the Z4 M Coupé.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: All ratings are provisional — no dedicated Engine Page exists for any E86 engine family. The M54B25 and N52 are well-regarded for their durability. The S54 is a high-performance engine that requires diligent maintenance — valve clearances need checking every second service.

overview.intro: The E86 is the coupé version of the first-generation BMW Z4, produced from April 2006 to November 2008. It succeeded the Z3 Coupé and was built on the same platform as the E85 Roadster, sharing its chassis and underpinnings but with a fixed roof that added structural rigidity and sharpened the handling. The engine line‑up was exclusively straight-six petrol: the 2.5-litre M54, the 3.0-litre N52 in two states of tune, and the S54 from the E46 M3 that powered the flagship Z4 M Coupé. Production was limited to approximately 17,000 coupés worldwide, making it a rare and sought-after modern classic. The E86 was replaced by the E89 Z4 in 2009.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW E86 (front-engine, rear-wheel drive) • Predecessor: BMW Z3 Coupé (E36/7) • Successor: BMW Z4 (E89) • Sibling body styles in this generation: E85 Roadster
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The Z4 nameplate generated limited UK engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED], with demand concentrated on the N52-powered 3.0si and the S54-powered Z4 M Coupé based on market patterns.

ownershipEconomics.h2: BMW Z4 Coupé E86 — Ownership Economics

rows.commonMajorFailure: Oil leaks, VANOS, electric water pump

economicsRule.title: The E86 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,000–£3,600 N52 recon against a £8,000–£15,000 3.0si sits at 13–45% of the car's value — well within our 70% BMW scrap threshold. For a Z4 M at £15,000–£25,000, a £3,000–£3,400 S54 recon is 12–23% — a repair decision, not a scrap question. These are appreciating modern classics; the replacement cost is a manageable proportion of the vehicle's worth. But beware of S54 rod bearings and VANOS — budget for preventative work if buying used.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E86 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: M54B25 (2.5i) — simpler engine, cheaper parts, good economy for a sports car.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E86 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: S54B32 (Z4 M) — valve adjustments, rod bearings, VANOS — all expensive.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E86 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: N52B30 (3.0si) — 258–265 bhp, sweet-sounding straight-six, modern reliability.

problems.title: N52 Oil Leaks & VANOS Issues (3.0si)
problems.recommendation: Have the VANOS system and oil leaks inspected at every service — a £500–£1,200 preventative repair is cheaper than the £2,000+ replacement that follows a failure. The electric water pump should be replaced proactively at 80,000 miles.

problems.title: S54 VANOS & Valve Adjustment (Z4 M)
problems.recommendation: If you're buying a Z4 M, budget for a valve clearance check and VANOS inspection immediately — £1,000–£2,000 is cheaper than the £3,000+ replacement that follows a failure. On a £15,000+ car, this is a cost of ownership you should budget for.

problems.title: M54 Cooling System & DISA Valve (2.5i)
problems.recommendation: If you're buying an M54-powered E86, budget for a cooling system refresh at 80,000 miles — £400–£800 is cheap insurance against a £1,800+ replacement. The DISA valve should be inspected for wear and replaced if rattling.

problems.title: Rusty Brake Lines & Electrical Issues (All Models)
problems.recommendation: Have the brake lines inspected on any E86 you're considering — a £250–£500 repair is far cheaper than a brake failure. Check the battery condition first when chasing electrical faults.

replacementCosts.h2: Z4 E86 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are E86 only. The E85 Roadster shares the same engines but is covered on its own Generation Page. The 3.0si was offered in two power outputs depending on market and specification.
